summaryForNext: Build-vs-adopt: BUILD/wire all 3 goals, ZERO new dependencies (no adopt/adapt candidates; library-candidates.json is build_required[] only). Design decisions RESOLVED against the authoritative Cedar Security reference: D1 (G1 reload error model) lint the merged YAML+DB route set at startup (bail!-under-strict, pre-serve) AND on hot-reload (WARN always; strict -> reject-route + retain-last-good, NEVER terminate a live process); D2 surface the merged Vec<RouteConfig> from router.rs:128 (built+discarded today) rather than recompute. D3 (G2) store validated sugar_policies as an immutable overlay on AuthzEngine and concatenate DB records ++ sugar in every build/reload path (from_database, reload_from_database at cache/mod.rs:372 + admin/mod.rs:727); config stays source of truth (overlay, NOT authz_policies rows). D4 (G2) precedence relies on Cedar forbid-overrides-permit (formally verified, source-agnostic under a merged PolicySet) — remove the db+sugar refuse-start guard (main.rs:306-315), test the permit/forbid conflict matrix; skip-on-error + default-deny validate the lenient parse-before-swap merge. D5 (G3) new admin endpoint {agent,allow,deny} -> compile_and_validate (same 400-gate) -> persist as sugar overlay; UI follows AgentIdentities.tsx (modal builder + RQ hooks) + Routes.tsx (nested structured config); MUST route operator input ONLY through the allowlist-charset compile_and_validate — the API boundary makes input attacker-adjacent (Cedar ref explicitly warns on string-concat injection). Build order G1->G2->G3 (G3 depends on G2's merged/enforced set). Each change touches authz/policy/reload -> separated security review each; G3's injection re-review is the highest-priority item. Open questions for Spec: overlay field shape (Arc<Vec<PolicyRecord>> on AuthzEngine vs threaded); one merged-set lint call at startup vs an additional one (avoid double-WARN); G3 UI as a second Policies tab vs dedicated route; document that the sugar overlay is fixed at startup (config sugar hot-reload is a follow-up).
